events.title: Me’ah Select: Can We Integrate Tradition and Modernity? (In Person)
events.description: Me’ah Select: Can We Integrate Tradition and Modernity? Understanding The Crucial Challenge of Modern Jewish Thought. Modernity was and is an impressive human achievement. History shows that modernity continues to develop, with unforeseeable complexity. However, that same history has also taught many thoughtful Jews that without some rich connection to the Jewish past — the lives of our ancestors and the great wealth of Jewish tradition — there is no Jewish future (and little Jewish present). But what can this “rich connection to the Jewish past” mean for modern Jews today, committed to modernity but also aware of its ongoing development? This course explores answers to this question. We start with Moses Mendelssohn, move on to existentialist (and neo-hasidic) thinkers like Martin Buber, Franz Rosenzweig, Rabbi Abraham Joshua Heschel, Jewish feminist thinkers such as Judith Plaskow, Rachel Adler, and Tamar Ross, and Zionist and Jewish postmodern thinkers, and end with a sustained consideration of Emmanuel Levinas’ unique “Judaically-shaped” philosophy. Dates: February 9, 23, March 2, 9, 16, 23, 30, April 13, 27, May 4,11 This course follows a sequential structure, and participants are encouraged to attend all sessions. If you are interested in joining once the course has begun, please contact the program leader. Financial aid is available. Registration: This event is available in person. Please click the purple bar above to register. Registration is through Hebrew College.

events.title: TBElle | Laughing Through the Ages: Jewish Humor, Resilience and Survival (Hybrid)
events.description: This engaging course explores how Jewish communities have long turned to humor—rich with wit, irony, and a keen sense of the absurd—as a powerful way to endure hardship and persecution. Through key moments in Jewish history, we’ll discover how laughter has lived alongside suffering, fostering resilience, offering hope, and illuminating the depth and spirit of the Jewish experience. Dates: April 28, May 5, 12, 19, 26, June 2 Join us for one, two, or however many classes you’d like to participate in! Registration: This event is available in person and online. Please click the appropriate purple bar above to register. This program is a part of TBElle (Temple Beth Elohim Lifelong Learning Experience). For over ten years, TBElle has been a vibrant community where congregants learn and grow together. This relaxed and fun experience is led by you—our fellow TBE members—who are eager to share their knowledge, experiences, stories, and talents. Discover new passions, connect with others, and contribute your unique perspective. Come participate and enrich our shared journey!

events.title: Interviewing J Street President (Hybrid)
events.description: While we are grateful for the return of the hostages and a fragile ceasefire in Gaza, we know the path to lasting peace remains long and uncertain. With a dangerous and escalating conflict with Iran and deepening threats to democracy here at home, the stakes—for Israeli and Palestinian lives, for the broader region, and for all of us—have never felt higher. Join us for an evening of honest, thoughtful conversation with J Street President Jeremy Ben-Ami and Rabbi Joel Sisenwine as we explore how we can protect democracy in the United States while advocating for peace, self-determination, and security for both Israelis and Palestinians. This is exactly the kind of conversation TBE is called to have—grounded in our Jewish values, our love for Israel, and our commitment to a just and peaceful future. Jeremy Ben-Ami brings a rare combination of deep personal connection to Israel and extensive experience in American politics and policy. Before founding J Street, he served as Deputy Domestic Policy Advisor in the Clinton White House. His family’s roots in Israel stretch back 140 years to the first Aliyah, and his father was an activist in the Irgun working for Israel’s independence. Registration: This event is available in person and online. In-person registration is through J Street. Zoom attendance is available for TBE members only. Please click the appropriate purple bar above to register. Co-presented with J Street

events.title: Author Event with Christopher Mirabile, The Washashore: A Seaside Mystery Thriller – Thursday, May 14th at 7pm
events.description: @ Please join us for a night of discussion and Q&A with Christopher Mirabile, author of The Washashore: A Seaside Mystery Thriller. The event will be held at The Book Shop of Needham on Thursday, May 14th at 7pm. “A thoroughly entertaining and suspenseful debut, from a writer to watch. The Washashore has action, terrific dialogue, and a charming cast of characters that will keep you hooked on this promising new series.” —William Landay, New York Times bestselling author of Defending Jacob A fatal late-night hit-and-run shatters the quiet of Provincetown, drawing interim police chief Silas Lopez into a case that appears straightforward—until the cracks begin to show. In a town where secrets wash ashore with every tide, nothing is ever as it seems. From windswept dunes and secretive cranberry farms to the gritty docks of New Bedford, Silas chases a trail of shifting lies—missing trucks, grainy night-vision photos, and a maze of half-truths—each step pulling him deeper into a game no one wants him to play. The deeper he digs, the more formidable the forces that rise to stop him. But Silas isn’t one to back down. A fifteenth-generation ranch hand turned plain-spoken cop, he’s fueled by grit, instinct, and a stubborn streak that won’t quit. With a young department still finding its footing and a growing bond with fiery social worker Wren Bradford, Silas presses on as the tide of danger rises around them. What begins as a tragic accident investigation unravels into something far darker and more dangerous, forcing Silas to confront a threat that could cost him everything he holds dear. Perfect for fans of Michael Connelly, Archer Mayor, and William Kent Krueger, this first Silas Lopez Mystery blends small-town intrigue, windswept coast beauty, criminal noir, and relentless suspense into a gripping debut that will keep fans of mystery series turning pages long into the night. events.title: Drum Circle: Spring 2026 (In Person)
events.description: Drum Circle start date has been postponed to April 16. Join us for an exciting, joyful drum circle with our own Friday night service drummer, Noam Sender. A drum circle is a centuries-old tradition tapping into age-old wisdom. Drumming is known to reduce stress and promote a sense of well being and mental alertness. Seated in a circle, we will play beautiful, accessible and captivating rhythms from around the world. The drumming will be enhanced with familiar chants and melodies (singing is optional). We will deepen the drumming experience with brief mindfulness exercises, learning to center ourselves, relax and explore our authentic self, while building a deep sense of community and sharing our collective voice. No prior experience required. Drums are available if needed, but feel free to bring your own if you like. Class size is limited to between 6 and 8 participants so it is best to register soon! This course follows a sequential structure, and participants are encouraged to attend all sessions. If you are interested in joining once the course has begun, please contact the program leader. events.title: Collection | Hunger Doesn’t Take a Summer Vacation
events.description: Did you know that the Racial Justice Initiative at TBE provides 80 TBE Table-prepared meals to students at the William Monroe Trotter School every month during the school year? These nutritious meals, alongside the school’s free breakfast and lunch program, make up a significant portion of many children’s daily diet. But as summer approaches and school year programs pause, that safety net disappears. No child should go hungry—and with your help, we can bridge the gap. Here are three ways you can contribute: 1. events.title: Adult Talmud Study | Daf Yomi (Online)
events.description: Join us for a practice of reading and discussing a page of Talmud a day, led by Rabbi Carol Mitchell. A complete cycle takes seven years! We meet on-line on Friday mornings to study the most interesting and timely Talmud tractates. Daf Yomi is an international practice to read the entire Babylonian Talmud in seven and a half years at the rate of one page a day. Tens of thousands of Jews study Daf Yomi worldwide, all on the same page each day, following a schedule fixed in 1923 in Poland by Rabbi Meir Shapiro, the founder of Daf Yomi, who envisioned the whole world as a vast Talmudic classroom connected by a global network of conversational threads. Learn more Come when you can. Walk ins welcome. Prior registration is not required. If you are interested in learning with us or have questions, please contact Rabbi Carol Mitchell. All are welcome!

events.title: Shabbat Morning Service (Hybrid)
events.description: Our Shabbat service includes the celebration of the B’Mitzvah of Nathaniel Fine and Sam Levitt. Registration: This event is available in person and via live stream. Registration is not required. Click here to join the live stream at the event start time. About Nathaniel Hello TBE, my name is Nathaniel Fine. I like doing a lot of things. One of those things is skiing. I love skiing because it gives me a chance to see beautiful scenery. Also, I like spending time with my family. I usually ski with my dad and brother and we meet up with my mom afterwards. Another thing that I like to do is fence. The many ways to maneuver my feet and my blade make me feel like I’m not constrained to many rules. My family consists of four people: my mom, Alisa, my dad, Aaron, and my younger brother, Jacob. I really love spending time with all of them. I go to Wellesley Middle School, and I am in 7th grade. Social Studies is my favorite class because I love learning about the past. TBE helps me feel connected to Judaism because at TBE I get to spend time with and get to know other Jewish people. To me, becoming a b’mitzvah means being a leader and helping others reach their goals. I also have a personal goal that I am working towards, which is to be more efficient with my time. For my b’mitzvah project, I created an interactive electronic lightboard at New England Sci-Tech, a science education nonprofit, to improve the visitor experience. The project included many hours of planning, drilling, electrical work, and installation. About Sam Hi everyone! My name is Sam Levitt, and I am so excited to become a Bar Mitzvah. Sports are a big part of my life, and I love playing football, basketball, and just about any game someone asks me to join. Playing sports has helped me grow more confident, step outside my comfort zone, and feel at ease in new situations. I feel very fortunate to have such a wonderful family. I have two great parents, three amazing siblings (Oliver, Emma, and Ruby), and two cute dogs (Daisy and Milo). My family has supported me in so many ways and helped me become the person I am today, and I am very grateful for them. I have attended The Rashi School since kindergarten and am now in seventh grade. Next year, I will be going to The Rivers School, where I will join my sister Emma. Through my years at Rashi, I have developed a strong connection to the Jewish community. In addition, my early years as a preschool student at TBE helped build that foundation. As I become a Jewish adult, I know that this milestone comes with responsibility. I hope to be someone who helps others feel included and who shows that even small acts of kindness can have a meaningful impact. For my service project, I am working with JFCS (Jewish Family & Children’s Service), which helps provide food to families in need. Every couple of weeks, my family and I organize the items a family has requested and deliver them to their home. Seeing their appreciation and knowing we are helping in some small way makes this experience very meaningful to me.

events.title: Mincha Ma’ariv Service (Hybrid)
events.description: Our Mincha Ma’ariv service includes the celebration of the B’Mitzvah of Jake Franklin and Taylor Merle. Registration: This event is available in person and via live stream. Registration is not required. Click here to join the live stream at the event start time. About Jake Hi everyone! My name is Jake Parker Franklin, and I live in Chestnut Hill, Brookline with my family. As the oldest of four children—and the first grandchild—I’ve naturally taken on a leadership role at home. My three younger sisters—Liv (10) and twins Gemma and Alexandra (7)—keep things exciting, and I always try to be a positive role model for them and for my cousins. I was born in Fort Point, Boston, where my parents and I lived until I was two. I began my education at Temple Beth Elohim for preschool, then attended The Fessenden School in West Newton before starting 7th grade at Noble and Greenough in Dedham. Geography is my favorite subject, and I was honored to be selected as a Prefect for my Sixie class. Baseball has always been a big part of my life. I’ve played in Newton Little League and Majors, and I now play on my school’s middle school team. I enjoy pitching and playing second base! I also love traveling and attending major sporting events with my dad, including the Super Bowl and The Masters—experiences I’ll never forget. This summer will mark my sixth year at Camp Winadu in the Berkshires, where I’ve built lasting friendships and even set an all-time record on the ropes course. It’s especially meaningful to me to be the third generation in my family to attend the camp. I’m very excited to celebrate my Bar Mitzvah on May 16th with my family and friends. Preparing for this milestone and learning Hebrew has been both challenging and rewarding, and it’s something I’m truly proud of as a young Jewish man. I’ve also valued the opportunity to give back by volunteering at Hebrew SeniorLife and raising money for Good Sports, helping provide athletic opportunities for underserved communities. About Taylor Dear TBE community, my name is Taylor Merle and I am so excited to celebrate this special moment with you. Playing sports is one of my favorite things to do. I love dance, gymnastics and hockey. I also love spending time with my family and friends! I really enjoy going to my summer home in Canada where I spend the summers with my grandparents, aunts and uncles, and cousins! I am the oldest of two children, I have one younger brother. His name is Bennett and he is 10 years old. We also have a cat named Blizzard! I am a seventh grader at Wellesley Middle School. I really like school because I like the subjects and I love seeing all of my friends! At TBE I have met so many amazing people. All my teachers have taught me so much and supported me through this process. And I love seeing all the friends I have made through Hebrew school. To me, leading with love, guided by purpose means choosing kindness even when it’s not the easiest option, and making sure that the people around me feel included, supported, and valued. It means using my actions to make a positive difference, even in small ways, and thinking about how I can help others, not just myself. Being guided by purpose means staying true to my values, making thoughtful decisions, and trying to grow into the best version of myself while helping to make the world a better place. Having my Bat Mitzvah is a huge accomplishment for me. I have worked very hard to reach this moment, and I feel proud and excited to celebrate it. I am donating to the Enke Ju Enkoirien School, which I had the opportunity to visit while I was in Nairobi this past February. Seeing the school in person made this project especially meaningful to me, and it inspired me to want to give back and support the students there. Becoming a Bat Mitzvah is not just about this day, but about continuing to help others and make a difference in my community and beyond.

events.description: Join us for a joyful and sacred community moment as we launch Scribing Our Story and begin writing the first letters in our new communal Torah scroll. Rooted in TBE’s commitment to connection, lifelong learning, and shared sacred purpose, this is a unique opportunity to help bring our collective story to life. Schedule: 9:15 am | Adult Learning: The Letter Bet Learn with Rabbi Sherman about the symbolism and meaning of the first letter of the Torah as we prepare to write Bereshit, the opening word. 10:00 am | Opening Celebration Gather as a community for music, ritual, and celebration as our Sofer (ritual scribe) writes the very first letter of our Torah. 11:00 am | Community Brunch Continue the celebration with a festive brunch and time to connect with one another. Come be part of this meaningful beginning—together, we are writing the next chapter of our shared story. Registration: This event is available in person. events.title: TBElle | Laughing Through the Ages: Jewish Humor, Resilience and Survival (Hybrid)
events.description: This engaging course explores how Jewish communities have long turned to humor—rich with wit, irony, and a keen sense of the absurd—as a powerful way to endure hardship and persecution. Through key moments in Jewish history, we’ll discover how laughter has lived alongside suffering, fostering resilience, offering hope, and illuminating the depth and spirit of the Jewish experience. Dates: April 28, May 5, 12, 19, 26, June 2 Join us for one, two, or however many classes you’d like to participate in! Registration: This event is available in person and online. Please click the appropriate purple bar above to register. This program is a part of TBElle (Temple Beth Elohim Lifelong Learning Experience). For over ten years, TBElle has been a vibrant community where congregants learn and grow together. This relaxed and fun experience is led by you—our fellow TBE members—who are eager to share their knowledge, experiences, stories, and talents. Discover new passions, connect with others, and contribute your unique perspective. Come participate and enrich our shared journey!

events.title: Exclusive Showing: The Day Iceland Stood Still
events.description: Progressive Needham and the Center at the Heights are pleased to present an exclusive screening of the award-winning documentary, The Day Iceland Stood Still. The film is an iconic depiction of a successful social strike. The day in 1975 that 90% of women in Iceland ‘took the day off’ rocked the nation and began a movement that has propelled the country from the bottom to the top of world gender equality indexes. The documentary uses personal stories and animation to show how this massive, humorous, and urgent act of solidarity launched Iceland into a global leader of gender equality. Stay for a conversation with Fredie Kay from the Women’s History Center and Deb Paul from Neighborhood United Indivisible following the film.

events.title: Wellesley Conservation Land Trust’s Spring Lecture—”The Role of Forests in Decarbonization”
events.description: How have the forests of New England evolved since the post-colonial period and how might they save us in the future? These are complex questions involving the ways biological, physical, and human systems interact to shape the region, including all the ways the landscape is changing due to both global forces such as climate change and local forces like land use. Central to our future is the role that forests play in decarbonization and the land use tradeoffs that may lead to net zero. Seated in a circle, we will play beautiful, accessible and captivating rhythms from around the world. The drumming will be enhanced with familiar chants and melodies (singing is optional). We will deepen the drumming experience with brief mindfulness exercises, learning to center ourselves, relax and explore our authentic self, while building a deep sense of community and sharing our collective voice. events.title: King’s Own Come to Needham!
events.description: The British Are Coming! On Saturday, May 30th Needham will once again host The King’s Own, a group of reenactors who portray the British Redcoats who sought to prevent the American Revolution. Adults, teens, and children are invited to attend the fun, educational event that helps commemorate the 250th Anniversary of the founding of our country. See authentic uniforms, weapons, and drill, ask questions, and learn about the challenging days that led ordinary people of Needham into direct confrontation with their King and his army. Learn how British soldiers lived, fought, and made their way though seven years of warfare from Canada to the Caribbean. This event is sponsored by the Needham Cultural Council and is free to all.

events.title: Shabbat Morning Service (Hybrid)
events.description: Our Shabbat service includes the celebration of the B’Mitzvah of Solin Meyers. Registration: This event is available in person and via live stream. Registration is not required. Click here to join the live stream at the event start time. About Solin Hi everyone! My name is Solin, and I’m a 7th grader at Dover-Sherborn Middle School. My favorite subject in school is math (although recess is definitely the best!). Outside of school, I love spending time with my friends and family—especially playing with my chocolate lab puppy, Vinny. One of my favorite things to do is travel. I love exploring new places, trying new things, and going on exciting adventures. I also enjoy playing sports. Over the years, I’ve played basketball, volleyball, soccer, and softball. People always ask me which one is my favorite, but I honestly love them all. Being part of a team—supporting each other and bringing out the best in one another—makes me really happy. If you asked the people in my life to describe me, I think they would say I’m energetic, caring, and competitive. I feel so grateful for my family and friends, who make me excited to wake up each day and see what’s ahead. For my ma’asim tovim project, I wanted to focus on something meaningful to me: helping people going through difficult times. After raising nearly $2,000, I started a project called Hope for Healing. With help from the TBE community, I created comfort baskets for kids in the emergency room at local hospitals like Newton-Wellesley. I hope to continue doing projects like this, because it makes me happy to know I can make a difference in someone’s life. Becoming a bat mitzvah means more to me than just leading a service. It means becoming part of a meaningful tradition and honoring those who came before me. TBE has helped me stay connected to my Jewish identity through its activities and the strong friendships I’ve built there. I’m so excited to celebrate this special day with all of you!

events.description: Our Shabbat service includes the celebration of the B’Mitzvah of Emma Andler Registration: This event is available in person and via live stream. Registration is not required. Click here to join the live stream at the event start time. About Emma Hi TBE. My name is Emma Andler and I am very excited to become a bat mitzvah. I am a 7th grader at Dana Hall School where my two favorite subjects are English and Social Studies. I live in Wellesley with my Mom, Elizabeth; my Dad, Rob; my little brother Jack who is ten; my little sister Lily who is six; and our two female guinea pigs, Figgy and Zippy. My grandparents, Susan and Julian Lange, live down the street from me, and we are very close. They are long-time members of TBE and my Mom grew up going to TBE and even used to teach Hebrew School here! Some things I enjoy doing are reading, hanging out with my friends, and playing with my younger siblings. I also love horseback riding, ice skating, and Broadway musicals. Singing and playing the piano are two of my hobbies. I’ve been in some musicals and even played Anna in Frozen which was all in Hebrew!! I have also helped with costume design in some other productions, including Mean Girls, Jr. and Legally Blonde, Jr. at Dana Hall. I have gone to Grossman Camp for many years and love it there too. I am so grateful to have met so many fantastic people at TBE. I even met some of my best friends here! Leading with kindness is very important to me. Kindness is key when leading if you want a community that accepts and includes people as they are. For my bat mitzvah project, I am serving as a Madricha (teacher’s aide) for a class of kindergartners and first graders at Kesher Newton and am teaching them Hebrew and Jewish values. Once I have become a bat mitzvah, I hope to continue teaching younger kids about Judaism and life as a Jewish person and I hope to continue to stay involved in the Jewish community as well.

Registration is not required. Click here to join the live stream at the event start time. About Avery Dear TBE Community, my name is Avery Frieze. I love to play all kinds of sports, including fencing, pickleball, soccer, basketball, flag football, golf and tennis. Sports keep me active, strong and it’s fun to get to know new people. I am the youngest in my family by a minute. I have two sisters, one is my identical twin and one is my older sister. I have two parents, my mom and dad, and one absolutely adorable dog named Ottie. She is a golden retriever and I love her so much. I am in 7th grade at Carroll School, but next year I will join my sister at Beaver Country Day. I like math the best because it is one of my strengths. I have made one of my closest friends at school through my parents and TBE. I really like going to Rosh Hashana and Yom Kippur services. I love all of the singing and how everyone’s voices are so good and how everyone is synchronized. I also like the speeches that are low-key funny. Being a leader in my community makes me feel more mature and responsible. Becoming a bat mitzvah means that I am a Jewish adult and I am responsible for living with Jewish values and changing the world by helping people. About Marin Dear TBE Community, my name is Marin Frieze. I am a soccer player and I play for my school, my town and my club. My family is the best. I am an identical twin, I have an older sister, two parents and one dog. Even though I am a twin, I am technically the middle child because I was born one minute ahead of my sister. I go to Beaver Country Day School and I am in the 7th grade. I like visual arts because it’s a class where we get to make sculptures and have fun. I love hanging out with my friends and playing soccer at recess, and I like math because I think I am good at problem solving. When I think about TBE, I think about my b’mitzvah tutor, Geoffrey. He is really funny and he has made Hebrew easy and fun to learn. He has been helpful and supportive by making sure I stay on track. I am so excited to be a Jewish adult. This means a lot to me because I get to become a leader in our community and set a good example by being a good student, a person who gives back and someone who stays connected to their Judaism. For my bat mitzvah, I collected clothing to donate to Cradles to Crayons and then went and volunteered to sort clothing and make care packages with outfits for teenage girls. It was fun to create all of the different outfits and package them knowing someone would open them and be happy. I also volunteered delivering food to the elderly with Family Table.

events.title: Slavery in Massachusetts: Then, and Its Legacy Today (Hybrid)
events.description: Massachusetts is widely celebrated as the heart of the abolitionist movement—but there is a deeper, more complicated story that demands to be told. As we mark our nation’s 250th anniversary, TBE’s Racial Justice Initiative invites you to reckon with a history that is rarely acknowledged: the relationship between wealth and bondage in pre-Revolutionary Massachusetts. The Royall House and Slave Quarters in Medford was the plantation home of the Royalls—the largest slaveholding family in the Commonwealth, with at least 60 enslaved men, women, and children—existing in painful tension with Massachusetts’ Revolutionary fervor for freedom from tyranny. Joining us is Dr. Kyera Singleton, Executive Director of the Royall House and Slave Quarters, who will share the stories of those enslaved in the Greater Boston area before Massachusetts’ landmark 1783 court decision to abolish slavery. Dr. Singleton will also illuminate the remarkable resistance and activism of the enslaved and free Black people of that era—stories of courage and dignity that deserve to be heard and honored. This is history that lives in our own backyard, and as a community rooted in the values of justice, tzedakah, and repair, we are called to engage with it fully and honestly. Registration: This event is available in person and online. Please click the appropriate purple bar above to register. This event is sponsored by the Racial Justice Initiative at TBE, which works to increase our understanding and activism to address ongoing discrimination and inequality. Coming soon: Registration for a mid-June tour of the Royall House and Slave Quarters—stay tuned for details!

events.title: Adult Talmud Study | Daf Yomi (Online)
events.description: Join us for a practice of reading and discussing a page of Talmud a day, led by Rabbi Carol Mitchell. A complete cycle takes seven years! We meet on-line on Friday mornings to study the most interesting and timely Talmud tractates. Daf Yomi is an international practice to read the entire Babylonian Talmud in seven and a half years at the rate of one page a day. Tens of thousands of Jews study Daf Yomi worldwide, all on the same page each day, following a schedule fixed in 1923 in Poland by Rabbi Meir Shapiro, the founder of Daf Yomi, who envisioned the whole world as a vast Talmudic classroom connected by a global network of conversational threads. Learn more Come when you can. Walk ins welcome. Prior registration is not required. If you are interested in learning with us or have questions, please contact Rabbi Carol Mitchell. All are welcome!

events.description: Our Shabbat service includes the celebration of the B’Mitzvah of Zoe Gilman. Registration: This event is available in person and via live stream. Registration is not required. Click here to join the live stream at the event start time. About Zoe Hi Temple community, my name is Zoe. I am a 7th grader at Weston Middle School, and I am very excited to celebrate my bat mitzvah on June 6th. This is such a special milestone for me, and I feel grateful to be sharing it with my family, friends, and community. I live in Weston, Massachusetts with my family. I live with my mom and dad, my brother Gabe who is 11 years old, and my dog Griffin, who is 6 years old. My family is a really important part of my life, and I love spending time with them, whether we are at home, traveling, or doing something fun together. I love playing sports, especially soccer and swimming. Soccer is my favorite because I love being part of a team, working together, and having fun with my friends while also competing. I also really enjoy sports with my family, and one of my favorite things to do is go to Patriots games together. Those are always really fun and exciting memories for me. In the summer, I go to Camp Micah, and this will be my 5th summer there. Camp is one of my favorite places, and I love being there because I get to spend time outside, try new things, and be with my friends. I’ve made so many memories at camp, and it feels like a really special second home to me. I feel very lucky to have all of these experiences and people in my life. I am really looking forward to my bat mitzvah and everything I will learn along the way. I am excited to celebrate this day and share it with everyone who has supported me.

Mr. Smith is the founder of ReEarth Solutions, a Boston-area ecological landscape design practice. He is also passionate about sharing his knowledge and experience and is the host of the podcast “Peace, Love & Pollinators;” the live, online, “ask the expert” event Tuesdays with Trevor; and the Hive-Mind for Business Owners, a program of the Ecological Landscape Alliance. events.title: Adult Talmud Study | Daf Yomi (Online)
events.description: Join us for a practice of reading and discussing a page of Talmud a day, led by Rabbi Carol Mitchell. A complete cycle takes seven years! We meet on-line on Friday mornings to study the most interesting and timely Talmud tractates. Daf Yomi is an international practice to read the entire Babylonian Talmud in seven and a half years at the rate of one page a day. Tens of thousands of Jews study Daf Yomi worldwide, all on the same page each day, following a schedule fixed in 1923 in Poland by Rabbi Meir Shapiro, the founder of Daf Yomi, who envisioned the whole world as a vast Talmudic classroom connected by a global network of conversational threads. Learn more Come when you can. Walk ins welcome. Prior registration is not required. If you are interested in learning with us or have questions, please contact Rabbi Carol Mitchell. All are welcome!

Anyone that has a mobility limitation and wants to experience cycling is welcome and encouraged to participate. The Friends of Natick Trails supports the All Aboard program. To register as a rider, click the link below: June 13, 2026 Time slots: 10am-3pm Meeting spot: 11 Mechanic St., Natick, MA 01760 Natick is partnering with All Out Adventures, who brings a fleet of adaptive and accessible tricycles and bicycles to Natick on scheduled Saturdays. events.title: Wellesley Trails Committee Guided Walk—Centennial Reservation
events.description: Join the Wellesley Trails Committee on a guided trail walk. Free. No registration required. Walks take place unless it pours rain or there are high winds. Dogs on leashes are welcome. DATE: Saturday, June 13 TIME: 10am-11:15am LOCATION: Meet at the Mass Bay parking lot off of Oakland Street, (not the Centennial lot) DESCRIPTION: Centennial Reservation (1.8 miles). Take a picturesque walk, through rolling meadows to the top of Maugus Hill, one of the highest points in Wellesley. View the beautiful Great Blue Hills in Canton from a hillside meadow overlooking the town of Needham. Then, descend through the meadow and around Benzanson Pond, a favorite swimming hole for dogs as well as frogs and tadpoles. Come explore this conservation land purchased by the town for Wellesley’s 1981 Centennial Celebration and now one of the most popular trails in Wellesley. This walk also includes Mass Bay land that is maintained by the Wellesley Trails Committee and the Wellesley DPW.

events.description: Join us for a meaningful and energizing day of service as we come together to support our neighbors facing food insecurity—an issue that impacts one in three people in Massachusetts. You’ll help assemble food packages at the Greater Boston Food Bank, a remarkable facility that distributes more than 100 million pounds of food each year across eastern Massachusetts. Experience firsthand how compassion, community, and action can make a lasting difference in people’s lives. Please wear closed-toe shoes; carpooling options are available to make participation easy and accessible. Contact the event organizers if you need a ride! Note: The Greater Boston Food Bank requires volunteers to be 16 years of age or older. (16 and 17 year olds require an accompanying adult) Registration: This event is available in person. Please click the purple bar above to register. Contact the event organizer, Bob Lamkin, if you have any questions. This event is sponsored by the Ending Hunger Initiative at TBE, which aims to strengthen and align the efforts of TBE’s many current and future lay-lead and organizational efforts addressing hunger through education, direct service, and advocacy.

events.title: TBE Music Club
events.description: Calling all music lovers! 🎶 Join us for a lively gathering where we’ll celebrate the songs you know and love, uncover new favorites, and connect with fellow music enthusiasts who share your passion. Come for the music, stay for the joy, discovery, and community! Remaining Dates: April 15: Cantor Shanna Zell will lead us through “The Journey of Women in the Reform Cantorate: Celebrating 50 Years of Sacred Leadership.” This is a program compiled by the Lowell Milken Center for Music of American Jewish Experience. May 20: Join Hal Slifer, popular WERS radio host, for Rock and Roll Memories!—a lively 60-minute multimedia journey through the music, TV Shows and Commercials, of the ’50s, ’60s, and ’70s. With rare video clips, fun backstories, and plenty of audience participation, Hal brings back the songs and moments that defined a generation. It’s a heartwarming trip down memory lane filled with laughter, sing-alongs, and great memories! June 17: A Special Afternoon with Cantors Jodi Sufrin & Roy Einhorn. Each course in this sequence is stand-alone, meaning you can attend as many or as few as you prefer! Feel free to join us for any session that interests you. If you have any questions about participating, please contact the program leader. Registration: This event is available in person and online. Please click the appropriate purple bar above to register. This event is sponsored by Music Club, where music lovers unite to learn more about music we know, discover new music, and enjoy a community of friends with musical interests. Come join us any time!

Couples and singles of all skill levels are welcome, from seasoned players to those just getting started. We meet weekly on Mondays from 1:30–3:30 pm for friendly play and shared enjoyment. Please contact the event organizers Jeffrey Arnold or Robert Nagle at least 72 hours before the event if you are interested in participating.

events.title: Text & Texture Ceramics Open Studios (In Person)
events.description: Our open ceramics studio is a space to connect and create in a relaxed and supportive atmosphere. Show up to make and connect alongside friends new and old We’ll use Jewish texts as inspiration for our work, enjoy snacks and drinks, and learn a wide variety of clay hand-building skills as well as decorative and finishing techniques. All pieces will be fired professionally in our TBE kiln. Feel free to utilize the full time or drop in for an hour or two. Whether you’re a beginner or have experience with clay, all skill levels are welcome. A trained ceramics guide will be present to support your journey! With your pass to our open studios you can drop in at any time during your chosen pass period. The pass isn’t limited to a set number of sessions; instead, it provides flexible access whenever you’re available. Since ceramics is an ongoing process that involves multiple stages—such as building, firing, and glazing—we’re unable to offer special pricing for individual sessions. Dates: April 6, April 13, April 27, May 4, May 11, May 18, June 1, June 8, June 15, June 22 Registration: This event is available in person. Please click the appropriate purple bar above to register. Registration closes May 23 at 11:59 pm. Members Fee: Sliding scale of $180 / $270 (suggested) / $360 Non-members Fee: $450 This fee helps us cover the cost of the kiln and materials. Bundled Spring Pass | Both Monday Evening & Wednesday Afternoon Times and dates as listed above. Fee: Members: Sliding scale of $300 / $420 (suggested) / $540 | Non-members: $540 This program is part of The Well at TBE, Institute for Integrative Health, Wellness, and Spirituality. The Well at TBE integrates Jewish spiritual practices and modern mindfulness tools and techniques to provide a source of wellbeing through learning, practice, prayer, and support for individuals on their own and in community.

events.title: TBE Book Club (Hybrid)
events.description: Gather with other book lovers to discuss a book each month that is chosen a couple of months before a meeting. Our Book Club is a warm and dynamic group of people who meet to share and discuss good reads, expand their perspectives, and get to know one another through lively discussion. All are welcome at any time! Be sure to keep an eye out for our monthly newsletter with details about the upcoming book and its author. If you are not on our Book Club list, and would like to be added, please reach out to Lynn Burke Harrell, especially if you would like to participate via Zoom. That way, you will receive the emails with the Zoom information for each meeting. Each course in this sequence is stand-alone, meaning you can attend as many or as few as you prefer! Feel free to join us for any session that interests you. If you have any questions about participating, please contact the program leader. September 16, 2025: The Love Elixir of Augusta Stern by Lynda Cohen Loigman (fiction) October 21, 2025: Treyf: My Life as an Unorthodox Outlaw by Elissa Altman (memoir) November 18, 2025: The One Man by Andrew Gross (fiction) January 6, 2026: The Last Kings of Shanghai: The Rival Jewish Dynasties that Helped Create China by Jonathan Kaufman (non-fiction) January 20, 2026: The Amen Effect: Ancient Wisdom to Mend Our Broken Hearts and World by Sharon Brous (non-fiction) February 17, 2026: Goyhood by Reuven Fenton (fiction) Book Club will be held In Person only for the February meeting. March 17, 2026: Fatherland: A Memoir of War, Conscience, and Family Secrets by Burkhard Bilger (memoir) April 28, 2026: The Boston Girl. by Anita Diamant (fiction) May 19, 2026: The Year of Living Biblically: One Man’s Humble Quest to Follow The Bible as Literally as Possible by AJ Jacobs (non-fiction) To RSVP, or if you have any questions, please be in touch with Trina Reinach at sreinach@rcn.com. This event is sponsored by Adult Living and Learning.

events.title: TBE Meditation Group (Online)
events.description: New to meditation and eager to get started? Or, are you practiced and want to join others for a collective meditation experience? Join our weekly live meditation group! We’ll meet either in-person, via Zoom, or both, creating a space where we collaboratively explore meditation—sharing what works, what doesn’t, and growing together. Dates: With the exception of the first Thursday of each month, when we will meet in person, meetings will be every other Thursday on Zoom: March 12, 19, 26, April 16, 23, 30, May 14, 21, 28, June 11, 18, 25 Registration: This event is available online. Please email Joe Bodoff jbodoff@gmail.com to register and receive the Zoom link. This program is part of The Well at TBE, Institute for Integrative Health, Wellness, and Spirituality. The Well at TBE integrates Jewish spiritual practices and modern mindfulness tools and techniques to provide a source of wellbeing through learning, practice, prayer, and support for individuals on their own and in community.

events.title: Adult Talmud Study | Daf Yomi (Online)
events.description: Join us for a practice of reading and discussing a page of Talmud a day, led by Rabbi Carol Mitchell. A complete cycle takes seven years! We meet on-line on Friday mornings to study the most interesting and timely Talmud tractates. Daf Yomi is an international practice to read the entire Babylonian Talmud in seven and a half years at the rate of one page a day. Tens of thousands of Jews study Daf Yomi worldwide, all on the same page each day, following a schedule fixed in 1923 in Poland by Rabbi Meir Shapiro, the founder of Daf Yomi, who envisioned the whole world as a vast Talmudic classroom connected by a global network of conversational threads. Learn more Come when you can. Walk ins welcome. Prior registration is not required. If you are interested in learning with us or have questions, please contact Rabbi Carol Mitchell. All are welcome!

As we gather in the sanctuary, we create space for connection and reflection, guided by our shared path to shalom. Afterwards, stay for our oneg and the chance to connect with each other. Click here to share lifecycle updates with the TBE Caring Connections team, letting us know if there is someone in need of a r’fuah shleimah, a full recovery of body and spirit, or other pastoral care. We would also love to celebrate joyous shehecheyanu moments with you, from the birth of a new baby to a retirement and everything in between. View this week’s yahrzeits Registration: This event is available in person and online. No registration is required to join us in person. events.title: Rules of the Ride—bike handling and confidence for 5th to 8th graders
events.description: Rules of the Ride is a fun, hands-on way to learn bike handling and confidence for 5th to 8th graders. This year’s event is Sunday, June 7 from 10 a.m. to 11:30 a.m. at the Wellesley High School parking lot. Participants will learn how to ride safely and how to legally use critical skills training from MassBike instructors. The event includes a guided ride led by the Wellesley Police Department. During the event, helmets and bikes will be checked for proper fit and safety before moving into brief group instruction and the guided ride. Participants need a rideable bike, closed-toed shoes, and a helmet. Registration is required. (use Activity #231505).

events.title: Life Review Workshop (In Person)
events.description: “They Are Our Life and the Length of Our Days” (from the Aahavat Olam prayer) Every life is a Torah—full of chapters worth revisiting, wisdom worth sharing, and meaning still unfolding. Rooted in the insight of psychologist Erik Erikson, who believed our deepest work in older adulthood is gathering our experiences into a coherent narrative, this workshop offers something rare and precious: dedicated time and sacred space to reflect on the life you’ve lived. Through conversation, creative exploration, and the grounding wisdom of Jewish texts, we’ll weave together memory and meaning—looking honestly at the path we’ve walked, the moment we’re standing in, and the road still ahead. This is more than reminiscence. It’s an act of wholeness. Come ready to listen deeply, speak wholeheartedly, and discover the Torah that only you can teach. All our paths lead to shalom—including the one that brought you here. Dates: Mondays, July 13, 20 & 27 | 11:00 am—12:30 pm Registration: This event is available in person. Please click on the purple button above to register.

events.description: Join Cantor Zell for a music project where we gather without musical scores to share our passion for singing, explore new melodies, and create spontaneous harmonies. Beyond learning and singing together, our aim is to cultivate a musically meditative space. No musical experience necessary to participate! Registration: This event is available in person. Please click the purple bar above to register. This event is a part of Summer Institute 2026: Stories That Shape Us, a month of immersive experiences centered on the creation of our community Torah scroll, Scribing Our Story. Offerings include hands-on workshops, text study, and conversations with educators and clergy. Engage at TBE, online, and beyond our walls, while deepening your connection to Jewish tradition and discovering your place in the Jewish story! Click here to view a list of all Summer Institute programs. We are deeply grateful to the family of Burton D. “Bud” Rose, M.D., who has underwritten the Summer Institute in loving memory of Bud, who was a lifelong teacher, learner, and lover of Jewish community.

events.description: “They Are Our Life and the Length of Our Days” (from the Aahavat Olam prayer) Every life is a Torah—full of chapters worth revisiting, wisdom worth sharing, and meaning still unfolding. Rooted in the insight of psychologist Erik Erikson, who believed our deepest work in older adulthood is gathering our experiences into a coherent narrative, this workshop offers something rare and precious: dedicated time and sacred space to reflect on the life you’ve lived. Through conversation, creative exploration, and the grounding wisdom of Jewish texts, we’ll weave together memory and meaning—looking honestly at the path we’ve walked, the moment we’re standing in, and the road still ahead. This is more than reminiscence. It’s an act of wholeness. Come ready to listen deeply, speak wholeheartedly, and discover the Torah that only you can teach. All our paths lead to shalom—including the one that brought you here. Dates: Mondays, July 13, 20 & 27 | 11:00 am—12:30 pm Registration: This event is available in person. Please click on the purple button above to register.

events.description: The Town of Natick presents All Aboard, an accessible and adaptive cycling program. Anyone that has a mobility limitation and wants to experience cycling is welcome and encouraged to participate. The Friends of Natick Trails supports the All Aboard program. To register as a rider, click the link below: September 26, 2026 Time slots: 10am-3pm Meeting spot: 11 Mechanic St., Natick, MA 01760 Natick is partnering with All Out Adventures, who brings a fleet of adaptive and accessible tricycles and bicycles to Natick on scheduled Saturdays. Staff will ensure a proper fit, and volunteers are available to accompany riders. All rides will start at the Mechanic Street lot on the Cochituate Rail Trail. This program is free to riders. Please note that advance registration is required for riders and volunteers.

events.title: Call for Walkers: Register for the 38th Annual Jimmy Fund Walk for Dana-Farber presented by Hyundai
events.description: Registration is open for the 2026 Jimmy Fund Walk for Dana-Farber presented by Hyundai. Scheduled for Sunday, Oct. 4, funds raised from the Walk support all forms of adult and pediatric care and cancer research at Dana-Farber Cancer Institute. The 2026 event will aim to raise $10 million in support of Dana-Farber’s mission to defy cancer by leading the way in groundbreaking research and lifesaving patient care. The Details: The Jimmy Fund Walk is the only organized walk permitted to use the famed Boston Marathon® course, and participants have the flexibility to choose from four distance options: 5K walk: Starting at Dana-Farber’s Longwood Medical Campus 10K walk: Starting in Newton Half Marathon walk: Starting in Wellesley Marathon walk: Starting in Hopkinton Along the route, participants will be motivated by Hero posters, photos and quotes from Dana-Farber patients, who are considered the heart and inspiration for the event. Walkers will also enjoy refreshments and cheer squads throughout the course. The event will conclude at the Boston Common Finish Line, by the corner of Charles and Beacon Streets. Finish line activities will include a celebration with food, entertainment, and more. Walkers who prefer to participate virtually can complete their chosen distance from any location. To register for the Jimmy Fund Walk for Dana-Farber or to support a walker, visit JimmyFund.org/Walk or call (866) 531-9255. Registrants can enter the promo code NEWS for $5 off the registration fee. You can also sign up to volunteer on Walk Day.

events.title: Civil Rights Journey 2026
events.description: Unanimously described as a transformative experience by the more than 200 TBE congregants who have traveled together on five previous Civil Rights Journeys, we are thrilled to announce another trip — and we hope you’ll join us. From October 22–25, 2026, we will travel together to Atlanta, Montgomery, Selma, and Birmingham, exploring not only the poignant history of the Civil Rights Movement but also the continuing and deeply relevant legacy of slavery, Reconstruction, Jim Crow, and mass incarceration in America today. This journey is a profound expression of TBE’s commitment to racial justice — a chance to bear witness, to learn, and to recommit ourselves to the work of building a more just world. Highlights of the journey include: Rosa Parks Museum Equal Justice Initiative’s Legacy Museum, the National Memorial for Peace and Justice (Lynching Memorial), and the Freedom Sculpture Garden Birmingham Civil Rights Institute The King Center Shabbat services at Montgomery’s Temple Beth Or Sunday morning services at Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.’s Ebenezer Baptist Church in Atlanta The trip is coordinated by ETGAR 36 (etgar.org), an Atlanta-based organization specializing in Civil Rights Tours for synagogues and Jewish youth groups. What’s included: All meals (Friday morning breakfast through Sunday morning breakfast — 7 meals in total), all ground transportation, hotel accommodations, and all admission and programming costs. Flights to and from Atlanta are not included. Frequently Asked Questions and Itinerary Registration: This event is available in person. Please click the purple bar above to register. Deposits due May 31. Registration closes and final payment due June 30. This event is sponsored by the Racial Justice Initiative at TBE, which works to increase our understanding and activism to address ongoing discrimination and inequality.

events.title: Adult Talmud Study | Daf Yomi (Online)
events.description: Join us for a practice of reading and discussing a page of Talmud a day, led by Rabbi Carol Mitchell. A complete cycle takes seven years! We meet on-line on Friday mornings to study the most interesting and timely Talmud tractates. Daf Yomi is an international practice to read the entire Babylonian Talmud in seven and a half years at the rate of one page a day. Tens of thousands of Jews study Daf Yomi worldwide, all on the same page each day, following a schedule fixed in 1923 in Poland by Rabbi Meir Shapiro, the founder of Daf Yomi, who envisioned the whole world as a vast Talmudic classroom connected by a global network of conversational threads. Learn more Come when you can. Walk ins welcome. Prior registration is not required. If you are interested in learning with us or have questions, please contact Rabbi Carol Mitchell. All are welcome!

events.title: A Tapestry of Faith: A Jewish Journey Through Morocco (In Person)
events.description: Embark on a meaningful journey through Morocco that reflects Temple Beth Elohim’s commitment to lifelong learning, shared experience, and the exploration of Jewish life in all its diversity. Together, we will travel through a land where centuries of Jewish history are interwoven with vibrant cultures, faith traditions, and enduring relationships across communities. As we explore Morocco’s ancient medinas, historic mellahs (Jewish quarters), and beautifully preserved synagogues and cemeteries, we will encounter stories of resilience, creativity, and coexistence that echo TBE’s values of connection, curiosity, and respect for difference. From Fez and Marrakech to Rabat and Casablanca, we’ll learn not only about the past, but also about contemporary Jewish life and the ways tradition continues to adapt and thrive. Guided by Rabbi Joel Sisenwine and enriched by scholarly insight and shared reflection, this journey invites us to engage deeply—with place, with people, and with one another. Along the way, we will experience the warmth of Moroccan hospitality, celebrate Shabbat in community, and open ourselves to moments of learning, meaning, and spiritual discovery. This is more than a tour; it is an opportunity to travel as a TBE community—strengthening relationships, expanding perspectives, and returning home inspired by the enduring legacy of Jewish life and the power of cultural exchange. Registration: This event takes place in person. Click the purple bar to learn more & join us.

We meet on-line on Friday mornings to study the most interesting and timely Talmud tractates. Daf Yomi is an international practice to read the entire Babylonian Talmud in seven and a half years at the rate of one page a day. Tens of thousands of Jews study Daf Yomi worldwide, all on the same page each day, following a schedule fixed in 1923 in Poland by Rabbi Meir Shapiro, the founder of Daf Yomi, who envisioned the whole world as a vast Talmudic classroom connected by a global network of conversational threads. Learn more Come when you can. Walk ins welcome. Prior registration is not required. If you are interested in learning with us or have questions, please contact Rabbi Carol Mitchell. All are welcome!
